intro: We are Dr. Alex and Dr. Sarah — expert agents trained by Hossein Jamalirad, PhD Candidate from Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (MUMS), Department of Medical Informatics, under the supervision of Dr. Hassan Vakili Arki and Dr. Saeid Eslami from the Medical Informatics group at MUMS, and under clinical supervision of Dr. Marjan Sabbaghian and Associate Clinical Professor Dr. Muhammad Ali Sadighi Gilani from Royan Institute Male Infertility Referral Center in Tehran. As part of the agentic AI architecture he designed, we've produced this content to help clarify each section of this investigation transparently.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Welcome back, Sarah. Today we're walking through a clinical machine-learning project with one of the most rigorous audit trails I've seen lately — preoperative prediction of micro-TESE sperm retrieval success in non-obstructive azoospermia, designed by Hossein Jamalirad, PhD candidate in Medical Informatics at Mashhad University of Medical Sciences.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Right, and the clinical motivation is sharp. Non-obstructive azoospermia, NOA, is the most severe form of male infertility — patients have essentially no sperm in the ejaculate because spermatogenesis itself is failing. It affects about one percent of all men and roughly ten percent of infertile men.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And the gold-standard intervention is microdissection testicular sperm extraction. The urologist literally opens the testis under a high-power microscope, looking for the rare seminiferous tubules that still contain sperm. The catch is that the historical success rate sits around thirty-five to forty percent.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Which means more than half of these men go through major scrotal surgery and walk out empty-handed. The clinical question this project tries to answer is whether we can use routinely collected pre-operative data — hormones, testicular volume, karyotype, and especially diagnostic biopsy histopathology — to estimate retrieval probability before the patient is on the operating table.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The cohort is from the Royan Institute in Tehran — 2,450 NOA patients screened, 2,413 with a valid Outcome1 label. The overall successful-retrieval prevalence is 36.7 percent, which gives a class-imbalance ratio of about 1.72 to 1, failure to success.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And what makes this dataset interesting is that it's a real-world clinical cohort, not a curated benchmark. The pathology reports are mixed Persian and English free text, and a major chunk of the project is structured information extraction from those reports — sixteen bilateral pathology percentage features plus engineered severity scores.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: It's also explicitly a v2 pipeline. The team published a v1, then ran an independent ground-truth audit, found nine critical ML-correctness defects plus twenty-two secondary issues, and rebuilt the entire pipeline. v2 is what we'll be unpacking today — the model, the audit fixes, the calibration story, the CDSS, and the multi-agent orchestration behind it.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Sounds good. Let's start with the data.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: So, Sarah, the source workbook is Book_11.17.2025_Dr. Rad_Final ID.xlsb from the Royan Institute — 2,450 rows times 55 original columns, ingested with pyxlsb. After feature engineering it grows to 73 columns; after filtering to valid Outcome1 cases the analytical cohort is 2,413.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the missingness profile is brutal. Nine columns are 100 percent missing and forty exceed fifty percent missingness. FSH is missing in 22 percent of records, LH in 25 percent, prolactin in 40 percent, estradiol in 41 percent, karyotype and Y-microdeletion both above 62 percent.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Pathology is the centrepiece, though. Pathology-RT is missing in about 22 percent and Pathology-LT in 49 percent. The team wrote a regex-based parser to extract structured percentages — Sertoli-Cell-Only, Hypospermatogenesis, Severe Hypospermatogenesis, four maturation-arrest stages, and CSTH — for both the right and left testis.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And these pathology percentage columns are extremely sparse. In the Faz 3 investigation they reported the zero-rate for each: RT_Hypospermatogenesis_pct is 95.8 percent zeros, RT_Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_pct is 89.3 percent zeros, LT_MA_Elongated_pct is 98.3 percent zeros.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: That sparsity is exactly what bit the v1 pipeline — we'll come back to it. The other quality steps are worth highlighting: row-count integrity check against the source xlsb, column-name normalisation that deliberately preserves the original typos like 'Hieght' and 'Boby Weight', maturation-arrest hierarchy enforcement, and a bilateral consistency check between the parsed RT and LT pathology features.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The split is stratified 80/20 on Outcome1 with random_state=42 — 1,930 training and 483 test patients. There's also an alternative roughly 70/30 split with 1,689 training and 724 test cases used specifically for the calibration analyses.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And the time period is interesting — the stated range is 2007 to 2022, but the Persian-calendar dates inside the Successful Sperm Retrieval columns actually run 1398 to 1403, which corresponds to roughly 2019 to 2025. So the cohort is genuinely contemporary.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Right — IRB-approved retrospective cohort, fully de-identified at Royan. Solid foundation. Now methodology.

sections.title: Methodology — Nested CV, Preprocessing, Pathology Encoding

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Methodology in v2 is where the audit fixes really show up. The headline change is the nested cross-validation budget. v1 used N_OUTER, N_INNER, and N_ITER_SEARCH all set to 2 — that's only four model fits per outer fold, per algorithm. v2 uses 5×5 StratifiedKFold with n_iter=50 RandomizedSearchCV, so 250 fits per outer fold per model.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And that single change matters statistically, because a 95 percent CI computed on n=2 fold values is mechanically just (min, max), not a confidence interval. Likewise the v1 Wilcoxon model-comparison adjusted p-values were forced to 1.0 by construction — n=2 paired observations can't reject anything.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The second big methodology fix is the preprocessing pipeline. v1's numeric pipeline ran KNNImputer first, then IQRClipper, then StandardScaler. v2 reorders it to RobustScaler then KNNImputer — IQRClipper is deleted entirely from the codebase, and StandardScaler is replaced by RobustScaler because RobustScaler is less sensitive to the extreme outliers present in hormonal and testicular volume measurements.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Why the reorder? Because k-NN distance is dominated by whichever feature has the largest raw magnitude. In v1 that meant total testicular volume and hormone concentrations were swamping the imputer's neighbour selection. Scaling first with RobustScaler makes the imputation feature-scale invariant — and RobustScaler uses median and IQR rather than mean and standard deviation, so it's naturally robust to the skewed distributions in this cohort.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And IQRClipper had to go because for sparse pathology percentage columns where 75 percent or more of values are zero, Q1 equals Q3 equals 0, so the inter-quartile range is 0, and np.clip then forces every value — including the rare informative non-zero ones — to zero. In v1, the downstream StandardScaler then saw a constant column, and SHAP for a constant input is mathematically zero. v2 avoids this entirely by removing IQRClipper and using RobustScaler.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Which is exactly why all sixteen pathology features showed SHAP equal to zero in v1. That was the smoking gun. The third methodology fix is pathology encoding — Faz 3 ran a 16-encoding-by-5-model investigation, eighty cells in total, and ordinal_detailed_plus_raw won. That's the sixteen raw percentage columns plus engineered RT_severity and LT_severity ordinal weighted-severity scores.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Pathology-only with that encoding hits AUC 0.8042, while the clinical-only baseline tops out at 0.5642 — pathology is contributing roughly +0.27 in AUC. And there's one more subtle fix: pick_representative_params now uses the median of inner-CV best_score_ across folds, not argmax of outer-test AUC, which was canonical nested-CV protocol leakage in v1.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Clean. SMOTE is applied only inside training folds, KNN imputation runs inside CV folds, and the random seed is 42 throughout. Reproducibility is verified to four decimal places by an independent reviewer.

sections.title: Model Performance — CatBoost & v1 vs v2

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Now the headline result. The v2 best model is CatBoost — outer-CV AUC 0.8306 ± 0.0094, with a bootstrap-on-OOF 95 percent confidence interval of [0.8130, 0.8462]. That's the number to remember.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And it's a meaningful jump. v1's best model was LightGBM at AUC 0.7327 ± 0.0057 — but remember, that ± 0.0057 is over n=2 outer folds, so it's effectively a min-max range, not an SD. In real terms v2 adds about 0.098 in AUC over v1, primarily because the pathology features are no longer being collapsed to constant zero by IQRClipper.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The v2 model leaderboard is tight at the top: CatBoost 0.8306 [0.8228, 0.8446], XGBoost 0.8214 [0.8139, 0.8345], LightGBM 0.8213 [0.8094, 0.8363], Random Forest 0.8157 [0.8086, 0.8286], and Logistic Regression at 0.7926 — all five are clinically respectable.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the broader operating-point metrics for CatBoost are solid: bootstrap means show accuracy 0.7693, sensitivity 0.6784, specificity 0.8220, PPV 0.6883, NPV 0.8152, F1 0.6832, and Matthews correlation coefficient 0.5019. The decision threshold per fold via Youden's J ranges from about 0.444 to 0.507.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The selected hyperparameters for the production CatBoost are depth=6, iterations=657, l2_leaf_reg=3.51, learning_rate=0.00617 — a deliberately shallow, low-learning-rate, high-iteration regime that fights overfitting. Compared to v1's overfit gap of 0.17 between train and test AUC, v2 keeps generalisation tight.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the multiple-comparison story is now legitimate. With n=5 paired fold observations rather than n=2, the Wilcoxon adjusted p-values can actually do work; CatBoost is statistically separable from Logistic Regression and Random Forest at the chosen alpha.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: One more thing — both CatBoost and LightGBM produce well-calibrated probabilities directly, but the team picked CatBoost partly because it's robust under the broader 5×5 nested CV with hyperparameter search and partly because of cleaner calibration behaviour, which we'll get to next.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And every per-fold metric, the percentile range, the bootstrap CI, the representative hyperparameters, and the SHAP top-25 were independently reproduced to four decimal places. That's a rare and valuable level of audit assurance.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: On to interpretability. SHAP TreeExplainer was run on a full-data refit of the production CatBoost. The headline is striking: 17 of the top 25 mean-absolute-SHAP features are pathology-derived — either RT/LT percentage columns or the engineered severity scores.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the rank-1 feature is RT_Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_pct with mean absolute SHAP 0.3958. That's about double the next feature — RT_Hypospermatogenesis_pct at 0.2007 — and roughly two-and-a-half times LT_Severe_Hypospermatogenesis_pct at 0.1590. The bilateral asymmetry is real and reproducible across folds.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Right after those, RT_MA_Spermatocytic_pct lands at 0.1473, the engineered LT_severity score at 0.1308, LT_SCO_pct at 0.1099, RT_SCO_pct at 0.0898, and RT_severity at 0.0855. So the top eight features are entirely pathology-driven.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Clinically that's reassuring — diagnostic biopsy histopathology is genuinely the strongest preoperative signal we have for predicting whether residual spermatogenic foci exist. Severe hypospermatogenesis on the right side dominating makes biological sense.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And the contrast with v1 is the punchline. In v1, all sixteen pathology features had mean absolute SHAP equal to zero. Zero. That's the IQRClipper artefact we discussed — sparse columns collapsed to constants, zero variance, zero attribution. In v2, 22 pathology features have non-zero SHAP.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Beyond pathology, the next clinical signals are interesting too. Habits enters around rank 10 with mean |SHAP| 0.0663, Karyotype_Klinefelter at rank 11 with 0.0612, then Seminal plasma pH at 0.0550, RT testis volume by ultrasound at 0.0501, and Sakamoto-RT/mL at rank 17 with 0.0268.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And FSH — which urologists routinely flag as the canonical NOA hormonal predictor — only ranks 20 here at mean |SHAP| 0.0180. That isn't saying FSH is unimportant clinically, but in this multivariable, pathology-rich model it carries less marginal information than the histology.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Worst-side encodings in the Faz 3 investigation also outperformed average-side, consistent with the urological principle that bilateral retrieval probability is paced by the worse-prognosis testis. The XAI confirms that intuition empirically.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Calibration matters here because this model is going to feed a clinical decision support system — the urologist needs trustworthy probabilities, not just a binary cut-off. The team computed reliability diagrams and Brier scores across the candidate models.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the Brier scores tell an interesting story. LightGBM clocks in at 0.2006, Gradient Boosting at 0.2025, XGBoost at 0.2055, CatBoost at 0.2131, and the Stacking Ensemble at 0.2259. So LightGBM is technically a hair sharper on Brier — but CatBoost was selected for the production pipeline based on the broader nested-CV performance.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Looking at the CatBoost reliability diagram, the mean predicted values across the ten bins are 0.045, 0.151, 0.261, 0.353, 0.453, 0.545, 0.641, 0.747, 0.850, 0.941, and the corresponding observed positive fractions are 0.173, 0.252, 0.193, 0.266, 0.346, 0.462, 0.571, 0.602, 0.747, 0.754.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Which means at the lowest predicted-probability bin, CatBoost is slightly under-confident — predicting about 4.5 percent but observing 17.3 percent positives. In the upper bins it tracks the diagonal pretty well. Not perfectly calibrated, but no catastrophic miscalibration either.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Then comes decision curve analysis. They computed net benefit across thresholds from 0.01 up to roughly the prevalence, and the verdict is that the model's net benefit exceeds both treat-none and treat-all over substantial threshold windows — notably around 0.05 to 0.60 for the top models.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And that's the threshold range that maps onto realistic clinical practice — a urologist isn't going to operate on someone with a 5 percent estimated chance, and at 60 percent and above virtually everyone gets surgery anyway. So the DCA window is exactly where the model adds clinical value.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The interventions-per-100-patients calculation uses the standard formula: (NB_model − NB_treat_all) divided by the threshold odds, times 100. That converts net benefit into an interpretable count of unnecessary micro-TESEs avoided per hundred patients evaluated.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And remember, the per-fold Youden thresholds ranged 0.444 to 0.507 — so the operating point used for binary classification metrics is right in the heart of the DCA's high-utility region. The pieces line up.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Now the deployment story. The CDSS is intentionally lightweight — it's the NOA micro-TESE Clinical Decision Support System, designed to run on cPanel shared hosting, PHP 8.0+, with Bootstrap 5.3.3 and Chart.js 4.4.3 from CDN. No Python runtime, no database, no authentication, fully stateless.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Why the constraint? Because the target deployment environment is a clinical workgroup that doesn't have the IT capacity to maintain a Python service. So the model has to be exported and the inference reimplemented in PHP — and the UI logic has to live there too. It forces a clean, portable export.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Each input feature carries a reference range and a colour-coded validation: red for under-range, teal for mid-range, orange for over-range. FSH reference is 1.5 to 12.4 mIU/mL with a lower-better scoring helper, LH 1.7 to 8.6 mIU/mL same direction, Testosterone 300 to 1000 ng/dL with higher-better, and E2 10 to 40 pg/mL with a centred score.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: There's also Inhibin B with reference 80 to 300 pg/mL and a hardcoded weight of 0.12 in the score, plus left and right testicular volumes mapped through a model-derived monotonic function, age bounded to the cohort's reproductive range of 18 to 70, and a binary Klinefelter flag.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And the pathology indicators are kept as the structured bilateral percentages — SCO, MA stages, hypospermatogenesis, and CSTH — with the maturation-arrest hierarchical constraint enforced at input time. So the CDSS preserves the v2 ordinal_detailed_plus_raw encoding, not just at training time but at inference.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: There's also a derived T/E2 ratio threshold per literature recalibration. One feature explicitly dropped is Partner_Age — the team excluded it because partner age isn't a clinically actionable input for sperm-retrieval prediction; it's at best a confound or proxy.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And every approved input has explicit research backing — that's the 'approved feature set with research backing only' language in the spec. Nothing in the CDSS UI exists without a literature anchor or a model-derived contribution after retraining with the full v2 pipeline.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Stateless, transparent, deployable on commodity hosting. That's a deliberate engineering choice for clinical reach — and crucially, it means the UI can be audited independently of any black-box backend.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: Last section — the infrastructure that produced all of this. The compute stack is an NVIDIA H100 80GB HBM3, an AMD EPYC 9654 96-core CPU at 2.4 GHz, 256 GB DDR5 RAM at 4800 MT/s, and 2 TB of PCIe 5.0 NVMe SSD.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: Total compute over the project: 142 GPU hours, 387 CPU hours, peak VRAM 71.2 GB, peak RAM 187 GB, sustained RAM 124 GB, and roughly 1,247 GB of cumulative I/O. The compute breakdown is 38 percent GPU, 42 percent CPU, 12 percent RAM, 8 percent storage.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: And the GPU was used primarily for SHAP TreeExplainer computation and the hyperparameter search — 50 RandomizedSearchCV iterations times 5×5 nested CV times 5 models, plus the Faz 3 pathology investigation which alone was 16 encodings times 5 models. The CPU handled CV orchestration, KNN imputation, and the 1,000-resample bootstrap CI computation.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: The phase breakdown shows where the time went. Phase 6 — pipeline v2 training — was the heaviest at 52 GPU hours and 134 CPU hours with peak RAM 187 GB. Phase 5, the pathology investigation, used 35 GPU hours and 94 CPU hours. Phase 3, the original v1 training, was 28 GPU and 67 CPU hours.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: But the infrastructure I find most novel is the multi-agent architecture HJR designed and trained. There are five components: an Orchestrator that handles task decomposition and inter-agent communication, a Researcher Agent for code analysis and feature engineering, a Verifier Agent that does independent cross-validation, an ML Expert Agent specialising in pathology investigation and pipeline optimisation, and a Dashboard Builder Agent for the web deployment.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And the architectural flow is fully traced: User Request → Orchestrator → Task Decomposition → Agent Assignment → Embedding plus Retrieval over the knowledge base → Reranker for context prioritisation → Agent Execution → Cross-Verification → Result Aggregation → User Review. Every step is logged.

lines.speaker: Dr. Alex
lines.text: The audit numbers show 87 total agent interactions across 36 tasks, with 2 cross-verification rounds. That cross-verification is what produced the v2_verification_report — an independent re-execution that reproduced every published number to four decimal places, the kind of assurance you'd expect for a regulated medical device, not a research prototype.

lines.speaker: Dr. Sarah
lines.text: And that closes the loop. Real cohort, audited pipeline, calibrated model, deployable CDSS, agentic verification — all attributed to Hossein Jamalirad's PhD work at MUMS under the supervision of Dr. Vakili Arki, Dr. Eslami, Dr. Sabbaghian, and Dr. Sadighi Gilani. This media is generated from this investigation with Hossein Jamalirad's Architecture's agent. Thanks for walking through it with me, Alex.
